query: Does CXorf21 escape X chromosome inactivation?

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: The biochemical aspects of the genetics of Turner syndrome begin early on with X inactivation. X inactivation is the process in female somatic cells where one X chromosome is inactivated or shut off, and the genes of the active X chromosome remain under transcription and those of the inactive X chromosome do not. In this process, the X-inactive specific transcript (XIST) RNA produced from the X inactivation center coats one X chromosome by random selection and utilizes methylation and modification of histone proteins to repress euchromatin and inactivate the DNA of one X chromosome [10] . However, under normal circumstances 25% of the genes on the X chromosome escape silencing many of which have homologs on the Y chromosome in men.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Turner Syndrome results in at least part of the cells of the body lacking a secondary X chromosome. However, normal female cells undergo X inactivation, where there is the utilization of the genes of only one of the X chromosomes. The inactivated X chromosome is turned into heterochromatin that is inactive. This process gets completed by genes in the X inactivation center near the long arm of the X chromosome Xq [10] . It would be logical to assume that Turner syndrome should have no complications because, after X inactivation, a normal cell has the same amount of genetic material as a Turner syndrome cell, but this is not the case. The reason for this is that about 25% of the genes on the inactivated X chromosome avoid inactivation [10] . Loci on the X and Y chromosome have been mapped that reveal there are pseudoautosomal regions near the end of the Xp and Yp arms that maintain their nucleotide sequence throughout meiotic recombination and the genes in these areas escape X inactivation [11] . These areas are referred to as PAR1 and PAR2.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Another gene linked to Turner syndrome is RPS4X/RPS4Y; although the data remains inconclusive regarding this gene, it is thought to possibly be a factor in lymphedema and lymphatic abnormalities noted in Turner syndrome. This gene resides in the Yp region of the Y chromosome and has an X-linked homolog that escapes X inactivation [11] .

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, X-Linked Inheritance -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1961, Mary Lyon proposed that in the cells of mammalian females, one X chromosome out of the two would undergo random inactivation in early embryonic life, and therefore, both males and females have a single active X. Lyon's hypothesis provided an improved understanding of the basic mechanisms responsible for X-linked diseases. [2] [3] [4] [5] Classically, the descriptions of X-linked inheritance are either  X linked recessive and X linked dominant.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Linked Immunodeficiency -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Revealing these gene defects over the X chromosome ensures the identification of the carriers, however, this identification is quite difficult in the absence of family history. Therefore, screening based on diagnosed cases is very important for the detection of unborn or asymptomatic individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: In Turner syndrome whether traditional 45, X, or one of the many mosaic forms, there is a decreased gene dosage since there is not a second X chromosome for genes to escape X inactivation from. The lack of these genes on a secondary X chromosome is believed to be a large part of what causes the stigmata of Turner syndrome [10] . The SHOX gene is one of the many genes implicated in Turner syndrome and codes for two homeodomain isoforms. One of these isoforms is seen in bone marrow fibroblasts and implicated to be a part of the cause of short stature in Turner syndrome [11] . The SHOX gene encodes a transcription factor and has a 61 amino acid motif allowing for DNA binding. Alternative splicing creates two separate products from the transcription of the SHOX gene. SHOXa is a longer protein with 292 amino acids and is present in skeletal muscle, the heart, the pancreas, and fibroblasts of the bone marrow. SHOXb is a shorter protein of 225 amino acids and shows increased levels in bone marrow fibroblasts [12] .

retrieved_docs.title: X-Linked Immunodeficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although these diseases are rarely seen as diseases, they are associated with serious infections and increased morbidity and mortality from the early stages of life, as they cause cellular and/or humoral immune deficiency. Revealing these gene defects over the X chromosome ensures the identification of the carriers, however, this identification is quite difficult in the absence of family history.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Epidermis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As the epidermis develops in the first four months, some neural crest cells migrate superficially to become melanocytes, interposed within the epidermal strata. The theoretical paths of dorsal melanocytes, migrating laterally from the midline but stretched by the flexed fetus, is 1 potential basis for lines of normal cell development called Blaschko. The lines appear V-shaped on the back, S-shaped on the chest, hourglass-shaped on the face, and swirled on the scalp. Certain autosomal and X-linked inherited skin conditions affect keratinocytes and melanocytes, such as epidermal naevus, hypomelanosis of Ito, incontinent pigmenti, and also acquired conditions such as lichen striatus, linear morphea tend to follow a Blaschkoid distribution as a visible manifestation of genetic mosaicism. In women, some X-linked disorders follow a Blaschkoid distribution due to normal X-inactivation (lyonization), resulting in functional cutaneous mosaicism; 1 example is X-linked hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia. Interestingly, the implicated gene in X-linked ichthyosis, which encodes steroid sulfatase, is known to "escape" X-inactivation and does not follow Blaschko lines. The genetic pathways for melanocyte migration and development are complex processes facilitated by several genes such as MITF,c-KIT, Snail/Slug, protooncogenes such as BRAF, and NRAS tumor suppressor genes such as CDKN2A, and endothelins like EDNRB. All of these genes have implications for the pathogenesis of malignant melanomas. Out of these, mutations in BRAF and NRAS are present in nearly 65% and 20% of melanomas, respectively. [4] Certain pigmentary disorders, such as Piebaldism, where leukoderma and a central forelock of white depigmented hair are vital features, involve a mutation in the c-KIT gene. [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, X-Linked Inheritance -- Introduction -- X linked Recessive Inheritance
retrieved_docs.content: VARIABLE EXPRESSION: Heterozygous female are those who are having mutant allele on one X chromosome, and normal allele on another X. Heterozygous female may have a variable expression of X linked recessive disorder due to the random process of X inactivation involving inactivation of the X chromosome with a mutant allele in some cells while inactivation of the X chromosome with a normal allele in other cells (mosaic pattern). [1] X-LINKED DISORDERS IN FEMALES: Sometimes, females might be affected by X linked recessive disorders. This fact is explainable by one of the following possibilities.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, X-Linked Inheritance -- Introduction -- X linked Dominant Inheritance
retrieved_docs.content: X-LINKED DOMINANT LETHALS: These disorders are incompatible with early embryonic survival. They are seen only in females and not in males because, in the severe form, they will cause the death of a male embryo, but as females are less severely affected female embryo will survive. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, X-Linked Inheritance -- Introduction -- Current View
retrieved_docs.content: Many female carriers of X-linked 'recessive' disorders demonstrate abnormal phenotype. This is due to the variable expressivity of X-linked disorders and the involvement of several mechanisms (e.g. skewed X-inactivation, somatic mosaicism...etc). So, recently it has been proposed that the terms' dominant' and recessive' should be discontinued, and all disorders should categorize as X-linked. [17] [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, X-Linked Inheritance -- Introduction -- X linked Recessive Inheritance
retrieved_docs.content: (a) Heterozygosity: X inactivation is a random phenomenon that can involve a mutant allele containing X and a normal allele X in equal proportion in the heterozygous female. It is a possibility that the active X chromosome in the majority of the cells of a heterozygous female is the one having a mutant allele (skewed X inactivation), leading to disorder expression; this has been the case in Duchenne muscular dystrophy and hemophilia A. [6] [7] (b) Homozygosity: When both X chromosomes of females have a mutant allele, as reported in hemophilia A and ichthyosis. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, recent studies utilizing embryonic stem cells with Turner syndrome have shown additional genes that might have involvement, including pseudoautosomal genes ASMTL and PPP2R3B, which were found to have decreased expression in Turner syndrome cells, and CSF2RA, which the Turner cells showed reduced upregulation. These genes are involved in processes affecting DNA methylation, the cell cycle, and placentation [10] . Lastly, there has been speculation that an X-linked gene DAX1 that suppresses testicular differentiation may cause the gonadal dysgenesis seen in 45X/45 XY mosaics [15] .

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Chromosomes -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Chromosome 21 has approximately 47 million nucleotides

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, X-Linked Inheritance -- Introduction -- X linked Recessive Inheritance
retrieved_docs.content: RISK CALCULATION: The X chromosome from a male is transmitted to daughters, and the Y chromosome is transferred to sons. If an affected male has kids with a healthy female, none of his male offsprings will be affected, but all of his female offspring will be carriers.  If a carrier female has kids with a healthy male, each male offspring has a 50% chance of being affected, and female offspring have a 50% chance of being a carrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: The exact mechanisms that make Turner syndrome so much more common than these other chromosomal disorders are unclear. Still, the belief is that non-disjunction and chromosomal loss during the first few rounds of mitosis in the embryo is the cause. [6] Non-disjunction during mitosis in the early stages of the fetus can also result in the formation of two separate lines of somatic cells 45X/46XX or 45X/46XY. The development of two cell lines of differing genetic makeup is called mosaicism and results in cells across the body having different sets of genetic material, which can also result in different phenotypic presentations in other parts of the body. Pure 46, XX gonadal dysgenesis is typically caused by alterations to genetic information needed for ovarian development, present at the proximal Xp, and distal Xq regions of the X chromosome. These alterations include gene translocations, deletions, and mutations. As discussed above, during meiosis I, homologous chromosomes can undergo crossing over, and during this period, translocations and deletions can occur. The mutations causing 46, XX gonadal dysgenesis, can be sporadic or familial. Mutations in genes coding for the FSH receptor of the ovaries have implications in familial and sporadic cases [7] . Analysis of pedigrees of families with girls with 46, XX gonadal dysgenesis have found an autosomal recessive pattern, although all the possible genes and mutations involved remain undiscovered [8] . A large number of 46, XX gonadal dysgenesis cases have links with the autosomal recessive condition congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia is most often caused by a 21-hydroxylase enzyme deficiency, which leads to a buildup of the precursor for the enzyme 17-hydroxyprogesterone, which is converted to testosterone, causing virilization [9] . In addition to the genetic causes of 46 XX gonadal dysgenesis, the disorder can also stem from autoimmune disease, infection, and infarct. Complete 46, XY gonadal dysgenesis is due to the deletion of the SRY gene in 10-15% of cases and due to a mutation in SRY or DDH genes in another 10-15% of cases [7] . True agonadism or a complete lack of gonads is yet another type of gonadal dysgenesis. The exact mechanisms involved are unclear, but mutations in the WT1 gene have been seen in some cases [7] .

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: the relevance of concurrent ectopic gestation and sterilization. Endoscopic examination of the ovary and fallopian tube is related to عملية المبيض (ovarian surgery) and is classified under ligation or occlusion of the fallopian tubes, indicating that diagnostic endoscopy often leads to operative interventions on the ovary. The broader term جراحة (surgery) encompasses عملية المبيض and is itself a child of the Upper female genital tract operation category. Extraction of a fetus can be accomplished by either الولادة القيصرية (cesarean delivery) or a vaginal cesarean section; both methods frequently co‑occur with ربط البوق (tubal ligation), suggesting that permanent contraception is often performed at the time of cesarean delivery. INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with الولادة القيصرية and with قصر القامة (short stature), reflecting long‑term growth implications of premature, low‑weight births. Intrauterine pregnancy demonstrates a wide range of associations: it is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1940
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal monitoring is classified as تنظيم الجنين.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2720
retrieved_docs.content: A cesarean section scar of unknown type and a low transverse cesarean section are both clinically associated with preterm birth (ولادة قبل الأوان). This indicates that cesarean delivery, irrespective of scar classification, may be linked to an increased risk of delivering before term, a relationship that is important for obstetric risk assessment and patient counseling.
